section.title: The Testing of Job
section.sectionRationale: This is the prose prologue that sets the terms for the whole book. Before a single friend opens his mouth, the narrator and the reader are let in on a scene Job never sees: a council in heaven where an accuser questions whether Job's faith is worth anything at all. Everything the dialogue will later thrash out is already decided here, in a conversation Job cannot hear.
section.thesis: Job's uprightness is real, yet Satan accuses it of being bought loyalty, faith for profit; God permits a devastating test to answer the accusation, and in the wreckage of everything he has, Job worships and refuses to charge God with wrongdoing.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: Job's blameless life and careful household piety; the council in heaven where Satan questions Job's motives; the fourfold disaster that strips Job of servants, flocks, and children in a single day; and Job's worship in the ashes.

units.label: Job's blameless life
units.note: a man who feared God and shunned evil

verses.text: There was a man in the land of Uz whose name was Job. And this man was {{tam|blameless}} and upright, {{yare|fearing}} God and shunning evil.
He had seven sons and three daughters,
and he owned 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, 500 female donkeys, and a very large number of servants. Job was the greatest man of all the people of the East.
Job's sons would take turns holding feasts in their homes, and they would invite their three sisters to eat and drink with them.

And when the days of feasting were over, Job would send for his children to purify them, rising early in the morning to offer burnt offerings for all of them. For Job thought, "Perhaps my children have sinned and {{barak|cursed}} God in their hearts." This was Job's regular practice.

units.label: The council in heaven
units.note: does Job fear God for nothing

verses.text: One day the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and {{satan|Satan}} also came with them.

"Where have you come from?" said the LORD to Satan. 
"From roaming through the earth," he replied, "and walking back and forth in it."
Then the LORD said to Satan, "Have you considered My servant Job? For there is no one on earth like him, a man who is {{tam|blameless}} and upright, who fears God and shuns evil."
Satan answered the LORD, "Does Job fear God for nothing?
Have You not placed a hedge on every side around him and his household and all that he owns? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his possessions have increased in the land.
But stretch out Your hand and strike all that he has, and he will surely {{barak|curse}} You to Your face."

"Very well," said the LORD to Satan. "Everything he has is in your hands, but you must not lay a hand on the man himself." 
Then Satan went out from the presence of the LORD.

units.label: The fourfold disaster
units.note: and I alone have escaped to tell you

verses.text: One day, while Job's sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother's house,
a messenger came and reported to Job: "While the oxen were plowing and the donkeys were grazing nearby,
the Sabeans swooped down and took them away. They put the servants to the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you!"
While he was still speaking, another messenger came and reported: "The fire of God fell from heaven. It burned and consumed the sheep and the servants, and I alone have escaped to tell you!"

While he was still speaking, another messenger came and reported: "The Chaldeans formed three bands, raided the camels, and took them away. They put the servants to the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you!"
While he was still speaking, another messenger came and reported: "Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother's house,
when suddenly a mighty wind swept in from the desert and struck the four corners of the house. It collapsed on the young people and they are dead, and I alone have escaped to tell you!"

units.label: Job worships
units.note: the LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away

verses.text: Then Job stood up, tore his robe, and shaved his head. He fell to the ground and worshiped,

saying: 
"Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away. {{barak|Blessed}} be the name of the LORD."
In all this, Job did not sin or charge God with wrongdoing.

tam.gloss: Blameless, complete, having integrity. It does not mean sinless perfection but a whole, undivided devotion to God that shows itself consistently in conduct. The narrator states it as plain fact before any test begins, and God Himself repeats the verdict to Satan; Job's coming suffering is never explained as punishment for hidden sin.

yare.gloss: To fear, to stand in reverent awe. Paired with shunning evil, it describes a heart rightly oriented toward God, not cringing terror but the reverence that produces obedience. This is the very fear Satan claims is only a business transaction.

satan.gloss: The accuser or adversary, here appearing with the definite article, so a title or role rather than yet a personal name in the way later Scripture uses it. He functions in the council as a prosecutor testing the sincerity of Job's devotion, and later revelation identifies this figure as the devil, the accuser of the brothers, Revelation 12:10.

barak.gloss: To bless, or, ironically, its opposite. In verses 5 and 11 the word is used as a euphemism for cursing God, too terrible an act for the scribes to write plainly; in verse 21 Job uses the same word without irony, actually blessing the LORD's name in the wreckage of his loss. The one word spans the chapter's whole question: will Job's mouth bless God or curse Him when everything is gone?

basic.context: Job is introduced as a real, exceptionally godly man, blameless, upright, wealthy, and careful to guard his children's hearts before God. Then the scene shifts to a place Job never sees: the heavenly council, where Satan questions whether Job's godliness is genuine or simply the product of a comfortable, hedged-in life. God permits Satan to strip away everything Job owns and every child he has, in a single devastating day, but forbids him to touch Job's body. Job's response is worship in the ashes of his losses: he blesses the name of the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter deliberately shows the reader what Job never sees. That gap is the engine of the whole book: the friends will assume Job's suffering must be explained by hidden sin, but the narrator has already told us the real cause was never Job's sin at all. The chapter does not explain why God permits such testing in general; it simply shows that He does, and that Job's faith proves genuine when it costs him everything.
basic.originalLanguages: Job is tam, blameless, a whole and undivided integrity, not sinlessness. Satan asks if Job fears God, yare, for nothing, implying his reverence is bought loyalty. And the Hebrew word barak, bless, does double duty across the chapter: a fearful euphemism for cursing God in verses 5 and 11, and Job's own sincere blessing of the LORD's name in verse 21. The single word holds the whole test in miniature.
basic.typology: Job's willingness to lose everything and still bless God foreshadows the greater righteous sufferer to come, who was tested to the uttermost and did not sin. The accuser who appears in the heavenly court anticipates the New Testament's fuller unveiling of Satan as the accuser of the brothers, finally silenced at the cross.
basic.christ: Job is an innocent man who suffers though he has done nothing to deserve it, and who does not sin in his response. That pattern anticipates Christ, the only truly innocent sufferer, who endured far greater loss and did not charge God with wrongdoing but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly. Where Job's faith is tested and proven, Christ's obedience under trial is perfect and becomes the ground of ours.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over Satan's access to Job; nothing happens outside a boundary God sets. He is also confident in His servant, holding up Job as an example to the accuser before the test even begins. This is not a God indifferent to suffering but One who governs it, even when His reasons are hidden from the one suffering.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 5:8 warns that the devil prowls like a roaring lion; Revelation 12:10 names Satan the accuser of the brothers, thrown down; 1 Timothy 6:7 echoes Job's naked I came, naked I will return; Luke 22:31 to 32 shows Satan asking to sift Peter, with Christ interceding for him.
basic.oneStory: The book opens with a test no one on earth can see, a hidden council that will shape thirty seven chapters of unanswered questions. Job's blessing in the ashes plants the book's central question, whether faith in God can survive when every reason to have faith is stripped away, and it points every reader forward to the one greater sufferer whose faith did not fail.

context.literary: The prose prologue frames the poetic dialogue that follows. Its formal, almost liturgical repetitions, the fourfold messengers, the twice repeated heavenly council, set a measured pace that contrasts sharply with the raw, broken speech of chapter 3 onward.
context.historical: Job is set among the patriarchs, outside Israel, in the land of Uz; his wealth is measured in flocks and herds and he offers sacrifices himself as the family's priest, in a way that predates the Mosaic law. The book addresses a universal human question, not one bound to Israel's covenant history alone.
context.canonical: This chapter supplies the frame that keeps the whole book from becoming despair without direction: the reader knows what Job does not, that his suffering is not divine punishment. That gap between what the reader knows and what Job knows drives the entire drama of the speeches to come.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish, before any debate begins, that Job's coming suffering has nothing to do with hidden sin, so that the reader watches the friends' arguments with better information than the characters in the story have.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes what happened in the heavenly council and in Job's household; it does not prescribe that God always tests the righteous this way, nor that grief must always look like Job's composed worship. The abiding truth is that suffering is not proof of sin, and that genuine faith can be tested and still hold.

debates.question: Why would God allow Satan to test a blameless man at all?

views.label: To vindicate genuine faith against the accusation that it is bought
views.case: Satan's charge is that Job's piety is transactional; only a real test could settle whether Job serves God for God's own sake. The test exists to answer a real question, not to punish Job.

views.label: The text does not fully explain it, and that unresolved tension is the point
views.case: The book never tells Job the reason, and it never fully satisfies the reader either. Refusing an easy answer here prepares the reader to expect none of the neat explanations the friends will offer later.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the test is not punishment for sin; the chapter's honesty about its own silence protects the reader from settling for the friends' tidy theology in advance.

debates.question: Does 'blameless' in verse 1 mean Job was sinless?

views.label: Relative moral integrity, not sinlessness
views.case: Job later admits the common sinfulness of humanity and offers sacrifices for his children's possible sin; the term describes wholehearted devotion, not flawless perfection.

views.label: A claim strong enough to rule out sin as the cause of his suffering
views.case: The narrator and God both repeat the description without qualification specifically to rule out the explanation the friends will insist on: that Job's suffering must be traced to some particular sin.

debates.bearing: Job's blamelessness grounds the whole book's refusal of simple retribution theology; his friends will be wrong to assume otherwise.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key wordplay is barak, bless: used as a fearful euphemism for cursing God in verses 5 and 11, then used in its plain, true sense by Job himself in verse 21, blessing the LORD's name after devastating loss. Job is called tam, blameless, whole in devotion, and Satan questions whether he fears, yare, God for nothing, for no reason but self-interest. The test exists to answer that very question.

typology.type: Job, the blameless man who suffers though innocent
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the only truly innocent sufferer, who was tested in every way yet did not sin, Hebrews 4:15.

typology.type: Satan the accuser appearing before God to challenge Job's integrity
typology.fulfillment: Revelation 12:10, where the accuser of the brothers is finally thrown down and silenced by the blood of the Lamb.

study.christ: Job passes an unimaginable test and does not sin, but his righteousness has limits, as the rest of the book will show. Christ is the greater Job: truly without sin, tested to the point of death, and never once charging God with wrongdoing even from the cross. Job's blessing in the ashes, the LORD gave and the LORD has taken away, is a shadow of a deeper trust that Christ modeled perfectly in Gethsemane, not my will but Yours be done, and that trust became the ground on which sinners can be accepted before God.
study.god: God is sovereign over Satan's access to Job, setting a firm boundary he cannot cross. He is also confident enough in His servant to hold him up before the accuser, Have you considered My servant Job. This is not a distant or careless God; He governs even the permission given to evil, and the chapter never suggests He is caught off guard by what follows.

crossReferences.note: The devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour, echoing Satan's roaming through the earth in Job 1:7.
Satan is named the accuser of the brothers, cast down, the same accusing role he plays before God in Job's council.
We brought nothing into the world, and we cannot take anything out of it, echoing Job's naked I came, naked I will return.
Satan asks to sift Peter like wheat; Christ's intercession, unlike the silence Job faces, is spoken aloud to the disciple.

study.oneStory: The book of Job opens with a test hidden from the man being tested, and a question that will not be answered for thirty seven chapters: is Job's faith real, or bought? His worship in the wreckage, the LORD gave and the LORD has taken away, sets the terms for every page that follows and points forward to the greater righteous sufferer whose faith did not fail even at the cross.

sermon.title: Questions of Suffering
sermon.why: Keller works through Job 1:8-22 to show that the book refuses pat answers to suffering, calls believers to embrace living without an explanation, and points forward to the ultimate answer found in Christ, matching this chapter's refusal of easy theology.

quiz.q: What does Satan claim is the real reason for Job's godliness?

options.text: That Job's fear of God is bought loyalty, dependent on God's blessings, and would collapse if they were removed.
options.note: This is exactly Satan's challenge in verses 9 to 11: does Job fear God for nothing.

options.text: That Job only serves God because his life has been comfortable and untested so far.
options.note: Close to Satan's real charge, but less precise than naming it as bought loyalty tied directly to God's blessings.

options.text: That Job is secretly guilty of some hidden sin.
options.note: Satan never accuses Job of sin; his charge is about motive, not hidden guilt, though this confusion anticipates the friends' later argument.

options.text: That Job does not really believe God exists.
options.note: Satan does not question Job's belief, only whether his devotion is self-interested.

quiz.q: What boundary does God place on Satan's test?

options.text: Satan may take everything Job has, but he must not lay a hand on Job himself.
options.note: Verse 12 states this limit precisely; God's sovereignty governs even the scope of the test.

options.text: Satan is given wide freedom to attack Job's possessions and family, but God still keeps him on a leash.
options.note: Captures the general truth of a bounded permission, though less exact than naming the specific limit stated in verse 12.

options.text: Satan may harm Job's body but not take his possessions.
options.note: This reverses the actual boundary set in chapter 1.

options.text: Satan may do anything he likes without restriction.
options.note: The text is explicit that Satan operates only within a limit God sets.

quiz.q: How does the narrator evaluate Job's response to his catastrophic losses?

options.text: In all this, Job did not sin or charge God with wrongdoing.
options.note: Verse 22 gives the narrator's own verdict on Job's worship and words.

options.text: Job tore his robe, shaved his head, and wept in grief, but still fell down and worshiped.
options.note: A true description of Job's visible grief, though it stops short of the narrator's explicit verdict that he did not sin.

options.text: Job sinned in his heart but not in his words.
options.note: The text makes no such distinction here; that nuance appears later, after the second test in chapter 2.

options.text: Job cursed God as his wife would later suggest.
options.note: This is the opposite of what happens; Job blesses the LORD's name instead.

quiz.q: Why does the narrator let the reader see the heavenly council when Job himself never learns of it?

options.text: So the reader knows Job's suffering is not caused by his sin, which sharpens the irony when the friends later assume it is.
options.note: This gap between what the reader knows and what Job's friends will assume drives the whole book's tension.

options.text: So the reader can watch the coming debate with more information than any character in the story has.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but less precise than naming the specific irony the council creates against the friends' theology.

options.text: So the reader can conclude that Satan is more powerful than God.
options.note: The council scene shows the opposite: Satan operates only by God's permission and within His limits.

options.text: To prove that Job actually was guilty of secret sin.
options.note: The council scene establishes precisely the opposite: God Himself calls Job blameless.

quiz.q: What does the wordplay on 'bless' (barak) in this chapter reveal?

options.text: The same Hebrew word is used as a fearful euphemism for cursing God, and then, in Job's own mouth, for sincerely blessing Him, holding the whole test in one word.
options.note: Verses 5, 11, and 21 all use barak, moving from feared curse to genuine worship.

options.text: Hebrew sometimes uses a single word to cover both a blessing and its ironic opposite, and this chapter shows that pattern at work.
options.note: True in general, but less precise than identifying the exact movement from feared curse to sincere blessing across verses 5, 11, and 21.

options.text: It shows Job secretly cursed God despite what the narrator says.
options.note: The narrator explicitly states in verse 22 that Job did not sin; his use of bless in verse 21 is sincere worship.

options.text: It proves the text has been mistranslated.
options.note: The euphemistic use of bless for curse is a recognized feature of the Hebrew text, not a translation error.

questions: Job worshiped in the immediate aftermath of catastrophic loss, before he had any explanation. Where do you tend to locate your security, and what would it cost you to still bless God there if it were stripped away?
Satan's accusation was that Job's faith was only as deep as his comfort. Has your own faith ever been tested by loss in a way that exposed what it was really resting on?

The chapter never explains to Job why he suffers, and neither will the rest of the book. How do you sit with unanswered 'why' questions in your own life, without manufacturing an explanation God has not given?
God set a firm boundary that Satan could not cross. Does knowing that evil operates only within God's permission bring you comfort, unease, or both, and why?
The text says Job did not sin in what he said. What would it look like for you to grieve honestly, even angrily, without crossing into accusing God of wrongdoing?

translationNotes.note: Blameless renders the Hebrew tam, describing wholehearted integrity rather than sinless perfection; the same root gives the noun tummah, integrity, that Job's wife will mock in 2:9.
Cursed renders barak, literally bless, used here as a reverent scribal euphemism to avoid writing the word curse next to God's name; the same word is used in its plain sense, sincerely, in verse 21.
The Satan carries the definite article in Hebrew, functioning here as a title, the accuser or adversary, within the heavenly court, rather than simply a personal name.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 1:1-22. Minor stray spaces before punctuation in the source were cleaned. No em or en dashes appear in this chapter's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file.
